item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm torn on this one. On the one hand, I like that it references the "Pluto is not a planet" thing and that we have a space monstrosity that is only threatening Earth because of the containment breach it would cause if it was detected. On the other hand, I couldn't help thinking about a Doctor Who episode with a very similar concept (and which aired before this SCP was written). So no vote, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A good reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Congrats! You have created the only SCP I consider to be non-headcanon! -1

I'm not only down voting because it's not in my preferred headcanon, but because it's just… meh. The idea of a giant space jellyfish replacing Pluto is just too ridiculous for me. Plus; New Horizons.

So, yeah… no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Fortunately as with most NASA missions, public interest is sufficiently low, so it is considered to be of little risk."

Sickest burn I've seen in a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
( ° _ʖ °)

How many SCPs do we have of space objects?

lets see.

This one.

The Destroyer

Giant dark space object.

The Hateful Star

KIC

Earth Rip-off

Other than that, I cannot give critisism or I could get banned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeap, this is obviously a piece of not-bad SCP, but I want Pluto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's also this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"assigned informal names to region " from the article, emphasis mine.

I doubt Cthulhu is going to stick.

For one thing it's missing a planetological descriptor like Regio, Mare, or Planum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, they named it Cthulhu?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that bothers me less than Cthulhu. I mean, find something twisty and tentacly if you insist on calling something that. Whales are Leviathan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey its cool. I think I am incapable of understanding why people are upset about it or what they would prefer instead. I have even watched Tyson's film about it…

I hope you can get over being sad to appreciate the awesome science that we are getting!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "heart" is the Tombaugh Regio, but I feel that Clyde earned this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, you're really not picking up what I'm laying down here, and I don't think this is going to go anywhere. We are speaking different languages here.

Also, dorf is a legitimate term for bearded fellows who live in fortresses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't find that dwarf planets are less mysterious. If anything they are more so. KBOs like Pluto extra so. We have no idea right now why Pluto and Charon have such smooth surfaces and probably won't have much more than guesses for a very long time.

From a writer's perspective, dwarf planets are pretty interesting. Along with moons they are the only places in the outer system that people or any reasonable robots could actually live and work. They are also the most likely places for things like crashed ships (or to be ships) or alien outposts.

Maybe you'd respect them more if you stopped insisting on misspelling "dwarf". :p

the demotion of Pluto is something of a sad affair for those who remember when it was a Proper Planet.

I remember the discovery of Charon, but I wasn't alive when "planet x" was believed to be a gas giant (and neither I think were you). It isn't a sad affair for me, and I don't see how it is a "demotion". Planets aren't sapient, they can't "pull rank" or use the executive washroom. It is merely a recategorization, which only serves to highlight how interesting Pluto is. It isn't the last planet but rather the first KBO. It is the beginning, not the end of a frontier of unexplored territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You seem to be missing what I'm saying.

Cause I'm not saying that the current definition isn't valid. Nor am I saying that all the other dorf planets get to be planets.

I am saying that from a purely subjective viewpoint of space-mythology, the demotion of Pluto is something of a sad affair for those who remember when it was a Proper Planet. This is all the bonuses that go along with being a planet, such as being Important and having a Mystique and all of that. All of those qualitative, narrative add-ons to being a planet.

I'm approaching this as a writer, not as a scientist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ceres was a "minor planet" in the IAU's usage until the same definition was created that made Pluto a dwarf planet too before that there wasn't an IAU but it's been an "asteroid" a "planetoid" but first it was a planet. If Pluto gets to be planet, then Ceres does too (and Eris, Sedna, Quanor and hundreds of thousands of
objects that don't even have numbers yet, much less names).

Do you prefer a definition that gives us hundreds of thousands of planets? Or one that makes a special excemption for Pluto with no scientific basis? Or what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah. Ceres was always a dorf planet, there's no loss there.

Actually, Ceres was a planet from 1801 until about 1860. Then it was "demoted" to asteroid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That sums it up rather nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah. Ceres was always a dorf planet, there's no loss there.

Best way I can formulate the feeling is that titles have a certain power in them, and getting kicked out of the pantheon stings. Consider it an analogue to the gods they are named after. Pluto got kicked out of the Olympus high-rollers club and is stuck being moon-tier. Sub-moon-tier, even. The spirit of the thing and the definition are clashing.

Also, not a fan of the scip, because there doesn't need to be an anomalous reason for the downgrade.

(It's okay Pluto you will always be a planet to me)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is it a downer? What does it matter if the object is defined as a planet or a dwarf planet? The object is fascinating regardless. Is the data from Ceres via Dawn less interesting because that object is also a dwarf planet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pardon me. I was under this impression for a few months. Rather a downer, considering that we're discussing this during our most momentous exploration of its surface.

item.title: Re: Broken Masquerade!
item.content:encoded: Robert Voor, you're breaking my goddamn heart. Yesterday I was so excited for the future of humanity because of the NASA photographs. Now I'm ashamed of my species because of its reaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Either my internet is butts, or this picture is broken.

item.title: Re: Broken Masquerade!
item.content:encoded: Perhaps we should send them a link to this article and see what happens.

item.title: Re: Broken Masquerade!
item.content:encoded: *facepalm*

item.title: Broken Masquerade!
item.content:encoded: Foiled again by those pesky Truthers ;-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You missed my point completely. General awareness that the universe has these crazy things could be as detrimental as being affected by an anomaly. Status Quo is God is the Foundation's policy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not a big fan of skips so anchored into a real world thing; one thing is for the Foundation to conceal the existence of some random stairways or something, but concealing the fact the we lost a whole planet1 is something completely different.
(Short rant follows)
It's not so much about the resources it would imply (though it does factor); one of the in my opinion interesting things about the SCP Foundation2 is the fact that the things described in the articles are *conceivably* set in our own world. Yes, they're not, but they could be, if anomalous things were to exist. And making one so fundamentally shatter the connection to the real world breaks that immersion.

Sorry for the short rant; in any case this is a problem of how I engage with fiction (this fiction in particular), and it needn't be the same way anyone else does. It does mean that I can't really like this article though.

Footnotes
1. It's one in my heart
2. This applies to urban fantasy in general

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree that the focus on Pluto and the coopting of Pulto's demotion were highly problematic, and almost made me downvote. Blaming real, well-known events on the Foundation is tricky enough that it should be avoided the majority of the time, and focusing on Pluto did feel like a gimmick to get attention.

Ultimately, though, I still upvoted out of plain weirdness. The idea that Pluto is now a shattered debris field, that a space squid is leaving the solar system, and that other planetoids are going to hatch next, while pretty basic, was presented well here. It didn't cause me concern for humanity, but I had fun picturing it. +1

item.title: Re: Thank you!
item.content:encoded: Ayyy now you can get an author page!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could envision a case where I no-vote a space jellyfish. I don't find the concept of giant space monster that happens to look like a familiar form of life terribly interesting, but the wiki could use a greater percentage of space-based anomalies that are written decently from a technical standpoint.

The surrounding elements, however, seal my downvote. That it's Pluto, at this particular time and place, feels like an attempt to gin up interest based on current events. Gimmicks like that are generally the sign of weak writing and tend to leave behind dated articles. That element seems unnecessary to me. Especially with the instantly-iconic image being included at the end of the article.

Additionally, I have to imagine that the Foundation, as an organization considered by most of the writers here to be at least somewhat experienced in the art of information suppression, would be aware of the Streisand Effect. The "demotion" of Pluto from planet status was actually responsible for a great increase in interest in the subject, and is controversial among scientists and astronomers. There are songs now referencing it for chrissake. More than that, though, there were actual reasons for why that happened. Generally speaking, I dislike attributing to the anomalous what can be explained through logic and common sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apropos
Apropos

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was a pretty big problem, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem with "Kill the Moon" wasn't the space egg…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, but those people also want Ceres, Vesta, and all the Kuiper belt objects to be planets because the definition that they are pushing is "object that is neither a star or satellite of a planet that has enough mass to form a spherical shape" without the "must clear its orbit" part of the IAU definition (which still stands). There's no definition that gives us nine planets. We either have eight planets and hundred thousands of dwarf planets or we have hundred thousands of planets. Either way actually works for this article. The average person isn't going to remember the names of hundred thousands of objects and Pluto gets lost in the noise.

I really don't get the "controversy" here anyway if Pluto, Ceres, and all those other things are "planets" we'd have to call things like Earth and Jupiter "major planets" just to keep them straight and the end result is the same as calling Pluto a dwarf planet…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw a Doctor Who episode like this. It wasn't a very good episode.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It hasn't been. There are a few folks at NASA who think it should be, but the IAU still says it's not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Source? My google-fu failed me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are professionals in disturbingly high places that espouse some…esoteric…theories. I can't find it anymore and I don't remember too many of the details (it was years back when I found it the first time), but there was a website filled with some high-grade ranting by a tenured chemistry professor at a major university describing a conspiracy by a combination of the US government and the International Council For Science to suppress his "novel" (read: thoroughly disproven and mildly insane) theories about chemical bonding, cold fusion, and the explanations behind various principals of physics. He also had a lot of gripes with IP, copyright, and patent laws… as in, he didn't think there should be any.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…I don't like this. It rubs me the wrong way.

I don't like large real world events being explained by the SCP Foundation. It feels a little too self-important to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only problem I have with this is that Pluto was just reinstated as a planet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 from me, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is it that whenever there's a non humanoid alien, it's almost always a jellyfish instead? I mean, first 321 and now this. What is it about jellyfish that screams alien to us? Nonetheless this is a pretty fun read and the idea of dwarf planets being eggs just fascinates me. Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea that New Horizons might be sending falsified data back is considerably more interesting than using an anomaly to explain why Pluto is now a "dwarf planet". The big problem with this, in my estimation, is that it's very much a "here and now" kind of anomaly. Is this going to have the same meta effect in a year? In five? Not downvoting only because I appreciate the names of the satellites. (And going to Wikipedia to look them up was a bit of a trip, given today's featured article.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Confusion and panic are some of the main things the Foundation tries to avoid. They protect two things: human lives and normalcy. Space jellies are a huge threat to normalcy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read a short story about this in Fantasy and Science Fiction a few years ago.

Also, it's silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Object classes indicate difficulty of containment, not inherent danger of the anomaly being contained. Yes, most Keter class objects are world-ending horrors, but not all. Some are just really, really hard to keep from breaking the masquerade.

That said, the object in question here isn't the remains of Pluto. It's the planetary-scale jellyfish, of which there are more eggs hanging out, waiting to hatch in the Kuiper belt. So we got lucky this time and it headed out for Barnard's Star. What happens when, say, Eris hatches and it strikes out for a different star at 30 km/s and, oops, collides with the Earth? Just because the danger doesn't grab you by the face and scream your name, doesn't mean it isn't a potential threat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spooky coincidence. 8o
Strange minds think alike I suppose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But each one of those has a hook that makes them potentially dangerous.

2460 is a mass of dark matter. If it does something that dark matter does that we have no understanding of, possible world ending.

1812 is a memetic hazard that if people are aware of it, bad shit happens.

2362 is a worm in the sky that is going away from us. It poses no threat and if word gets out that Pluto is gone, then what? Confusion and panic maybe, but not world ending. It's not Keter to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By that logic, SCP-2557 shouldn't be Keter either. Potential information breaches regarding the Foundation or the existence of the anomalous are just as important to contain because even if the object itself doesn't have devastating implications on humanity, public knowledge of things that make the laws of nature look like a bad joke very well might. People see a space jellyfish, and they start looking harder. Soon they find a mass of dark matter orbiting Earth, a memetic second moon, and then the whole charade falls apart. The Foundation works to preserve normalcy, as anything else is inimical to the existence of humanity as we know it (whether or not that's always the most noble goal).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
■Keter class SCPs require extremely specific containment procedures, and are simply so difficult to contain effectively that they need higher levels of care and resources to suppress. If you lock it in a box, and there's a chance it bursts out through the lid of its own accord unless you follow very stringent containment procedures, it's Keter. A Keter class SCP needs to stand out from other articles and be a unique threat all its own. This class includes Shadow Person, An Incomplete Chronicle, and The Maybe There Monsters.

Yes, but being difficult to contain is only part of it. It doesn't pose a unique threat. SCP-1548 (a celestial body as well) does both. It is difficult to contain (prevent knowledge to population) and poses a unique threat (oh shit, it's coming to get us). This SCP is only half that. That's why I stated that it needs more to be Keter. That's why I suggested the asteroid belt. Hell, make a moon of Mars an instance with indication that it may hatch soon. Use a model of the Pluto instance's exit path to show a possible collision course with Earth. There is so much that can be done to make it really feel Keter, but it just doesn't do it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clicks on link to website about how the Roswell crash was a Nazi time travel experiment.

Do I see what you did there, or is that a sp00ky coincidence at work?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keter doesn't mean "Oh god we're doomed". It means extremely difficult to contain and could reveal the anomalous to the rest of the world. That hard-to-contain property is frequently a result of potential for global devastation, but it's not a requirement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fortunately as with most NASA missions, public interest is sufficiently low, so it is considered to be of little risk.

Incidentally, check out today's Google Doodle. Yeah, everything is Pluto today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you've got a decent idea here, but there's a few things keeping me from upvoting.

Fortunately as with most NASA missions, public interest is sufficiently low, so it is considered to be of little risk.

I don't like this line. Tonally, it's off, and while I get that this whole thing has a sense of tongue-in-cheek throughout, this just pushes it a bit too far.

I'm also not sold on the containment procedures. It's done, Pluto is gone. Why would the Foundation perpetually sink resources into keeping everyone thinking it's there when they could say something like "New Horizons has detected a large object on a collision course with Pluto coming from the outer Kuiper Belt" and transition to the contingency plan within in the next few years? Better to get it out of the way now than wait until civilian technology has advanced to the point where they start asking questions so they have to say "Oh yeah, that blew up last year, sorry we forgot to bring it up"?

Other than that, I'm not overly fond of the fact that your hook is in the last three sentences, but that's probably a personal thing. I mostly like this, but those things are keeping me from upvoting right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh. Our solar system has massive jelly fish eggs that when hatch leave the system. I hardly consider that keter. It has shown no agressive behavior and pluto seems like its been hanging around for a long time.

You want to make them Keter? Holy shit, they are responsible for the asteroid belt! That big red storm on Jupiter? Some gas life forms gone done pissed off the great giant jellyfish in the sky! And what's the deal with Saturn's rings? I blame flying speghetti mostersssss-I-mean-space-jellyfish. They could leave some kind of traces to show that we should really look into what it would take to destroy any hostile examples. We know that they are there and that they can be extremely destructive. There are tons of things in this universe that are planet destroying and none of them have come close enough (whoops, jellyfish threw an asteroid at the dinosaurs).

I just feel that there could be more to make it Keter and more interesting. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No problem. Thanks for the suggestion!

That would be yet another hate letter I have to write to the IAU. And that monster Mike Brown. His twitter handle is seriously @plutokiller.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That really helps to make it feel better. Thank you.

(And if we don't end up calling "the whale" Leviathan and "the heart" Persephone, then the IAU can just go home.)
